edges.explanation: 7.6 million waiting is the headline evidence that lists cannot be cleared in one parliament
The scale of the waiting list directly demonstrates that manifesto funding levels are insufficient
7.6 million waiting makes Reform UK's claim of zero waiting lists in 2 years mathematically implausible
A&E performance collapsing from 96.6% to 74.5% shows systemic decline that cannot reverse in one parliament
18-week target not met since 2016 demonstrates structural underfunding, not a temporary blip
Productivity 11.4% below 2019 despite 17% more staff proves technology and efficiency alone cannot close the gap
Getting bigger without getting better means spending more has not produced proportionate outcomes
The productivity stall despite staff increases supports the framing that the system needs structural reform not just money
13% of beds blocked by social care patients is the measurable consequence of 25 years of failed social care reform
Bed-blocking is the direct mechanism by which social care failure drives the A&E crisis
Freeing 13% of hospital beds through social care reform would enable thousands of additional elective procedures without new capacity
£37bn capital shortfall since 2010 with 188% maintenance backlog growth demonstrates sustained systemic underfunding
Technology cannot be deployed on crumbling infrastructure — the capital shortfall must be addressed first
The £37bn figure directly sizes the capital clearance policy at £3.7bn per year over ten years
The capital shortfall is one of the six root causes Darzi identifies for the system being in critical condition
27% of nurses and 50% of secondary-care doctors trained abroad proves the workforce plan depends on continued overseas recruitment
The scale of overseas workforce dependency makes the care route closure a direct policy contradiction
The dependency data is the evidence base for the policy to maintain overseas recruitment during domestic transition
20% of primary care estate predating 1948 and being 15 years behind on technology means digital transformation requires physical investment first
Hospital nursing up 35% while community nursing fell 5% is the clearest evidence of the hospital-community imbalance the prevention shift aims to correct
The community nursing decline provides the evidence base for the Neighbourhood Health Service model shifting 20% of hospital budgets to community teams
2.8 million economically inactive with each return worth £25k/yr is the economic case for treating long-term sickness as an emergency
The economic inactivity figures contribute to the total cost of NHS underperformance beyond just the direct funding gap
£73bn economic benefit from clearing backlogs provides an economic justification for the additional NHS investment beyond health outcomes alone
The £1 saves £4-14 evidence is the cost-effectiveness basis for prevention being more efficient than treatment
The PHE return-on-investment evidence directly justifies the ring-fenced £1bn/yr prevention budget
19% non-UK social care workforce and the July 2025 route closure proves the workforce plan cannot deliver without overseas recruitment
The closure of the overseas care route while 19% of the workforce depends on it is the specific evidence of policy contradiction
RCN projecting nursing numbers halving by 2029 without intervention shows how urgently the workforce plan depends on overseas recruitment as a bridge
15,000-23,000 doctors leaving annually means the domestic training pipeline must overcome attrition as well as fill gaps
Mass doctor departures show that even funded training places may not translate to retained workforce without addressing working conditions
1 million people waiting for mental health services adds to the total backlog picture that cannot clear in one parliament
The mental health waiting list is an additional dimension of underfunding not adequately addressed in any manifesto
The inability to clear lists in one parliament is itself evidence that manifesto funding commitments are insufficient
The systemic underfunding verdict directly supports the claim that all manifestos fall short
The £20-23bn gap between commitments and requirements is the basis for the total £20-25bn additional need
The workforce plan's 15-year overseas dependency is what makes Labour's route closure a contradiction
25 years of failed reform produces the 13% bed-blocking that drives the A&E crisis
The evidence that prevention is more cost-effective supports the broader claim that the system needs structural reorientation
The 3.5% growth policy exists because all manifesto commitments fall short — if they didn't, this policy wouldn't be needed
The policy to maintain overseas routes depends on the claim that closing them contradicts the workforce plan
The £20-25bn annual gap directly sizes the 3.5% growth requirement
Social care's £5bn is a component of the total £20-25bn gap — social care funding is inseparable from NHS funding
Social care funding policy is justified primarily by the bed-blocking crisis it would resolve
Capital clearance is needed precisely because technology cannot work without it
Integrated digital records depend on capital investment in systems that currently don't interoperate
Prevention ring-fence depends on the assumption that prevention is more cost-effective than treatment
The Neighbourhood Health Service depends on the assumption that community-based care is more effective than hospital-centric care
Evidence that private healthcare expansion doesn't reduce NHS waiting lists at system level contradicts Reform's tax relief proposal
Conservatives endorse the workforce plan but their funding commitment falls far short of what the plan requires
Conservative £3.7bn social care pledge partially addresses but significantly underfunds the £5bn+ evidence-based requirement
Conservative technology investment partially addresses the capital need but at a fraction of the £37bn shortfall
Labour endorses the workforce plan and extra appointments but without the funding the plan requires
Labour's National Care Service and Fair Pay Agreement most closely match the social care funding policy
Labour endorses the workforce plan but closed the overseas care route the plan depends on — a direct policy contradiction
Labour's shift to prevention and 10-year plan aligns with the ring-fenced prevention policy direction
Labour's NHS App and prevention rhetoric aligns with but is much less ambitious than the Neighbourhood Health Service model
Lib Dems' £8.4bn is the largest specific pledge from a main party — closer to evidence-based need though still below it
Lib Dems' explicit £1bn/yr for public health matches the evidence-based prevention ring-fence almost exactly
Free personal care and carer's minimum wage directly address the social care funding gap
Green's £1.5bn/yr public health restoration exceeds the evidence-based minimum of £1bn/yr
Green's £20bn free personal care is the most ambitious social care commitment, closest to the structural reform the evidence demands
Green's call to end the distinction between health and social care aligns with the integrated community care model
Reform's £17bn figure is large but entirely uncosted and coupled with implausible claims about clearing waiting lists in two years
Revenue funding growth and capital backlog clearance both draw from the same constrained fiscal envelope
NHS funding and social care funding compete for the same public spending headroom — increasing one makes the other harder politically
Prevention spending competes with acute treatment funding in the short term, though it reduces treatment costs long-term
Maintaining overseas care recruitment routes enables social care staffing which is needed for the social care funding policy to deliver
20% of primary care estate predating 1948 is the evidence that capital clearance must prioritise physical infrastructure not just digital
The £73bn economic benefit quantifies the cost of NOT clearing the backlog, reinforcing that the lists must be addressed even if not within one parliament
Nursing numbers halving by 2029 makes the overseas route closure even more damaging — the domestic pipeline cannot fill the gap in time
Reform UK policy of tax relief on private healthcare is the specific party position that the private healthcare assumption tests
The claim that the system needs structural reform not just money means the 3.5% growth policy must come alongside prevention shift and social care integration
The long-term sickness emergency policy depends on the total funding gap claim because it requires dedicated NHS capacity within the overall funding envelope

cross_domain_edges.explanation: NHS workforce plan depends on continued overseas recruitment — 1 in 5 staff are foreign nationals, 15-year domestic training pipeline
Social care workforce (19% non-UK) depends on immigration routes remaining open — the July 2025 closure directly threatens staffing
The workforce contradiction claim directly challenges Labour's decision to close the overseas care worker route
Cold and damp housing drives respiratory admissions that contribute to NHS bed pressure
2.8 million economically inactive from long-term sickness links NHS waiting lists directly to welfare spending on sickness benefits
The £20-25bn NHS gap requires fiscal space that is constrained by tax locks and revenue limits across the whole fiscal envelope
NHS prevention spending addresses health impacts of air pollution, creating shared value between health and environmental policy
